coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of novel carbon quantum dots from biomass waste for highly sensitive detection of iron ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Shi, Huanxian
Yang, Min
Yan, Yujia
Liu, Enzhou
Ji, Zhen
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, some common and inexpensive biomass waste were used as carbon sources including orange peel, ginkgo biloba leaves, paulownia leaves and magnolia flower to prepare biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by using a facile hydrothermal process. These biomass CQDs exhibited the advantages of homogeneous particle size, superior water solubility and stability, similar optical properties and surface functional group. The fluorescence quenching effect was observed when Fe3+ ions were added into CQDs solution. The experiment results showed that the as-prepared CQDs have a sensitive response to Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 0.2−100 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) could as low as 0.073 μM. More importantly, practical applications for the detection Fe3+ ions in pond water acquired satisfactory recoveries (94 %–108 %). Therefore, this work not only provided a new way for reusages of biomass waste, but also provided a novel alternative sensor for Fe3+ ions detection in real-world.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass waste
Carbon quantum dots
Fe3+ ions
Concentration detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CQDs to Fe3+ ions and fluorescence quenching mechanism
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: the size distribution histogram) and (b) HRTEM of MF-CQDs, (c) FTIR spectrum of all CQDs, (d) XPS survey spectra of MF-CQDs.
High-resolution XPS of C 1s (a), N 1s (b), O 1s (c), and S 2p spectra (d) of MF-CQDs.
The UV–vis and photoluminescence spectrum of different CQDs, (a) OP-CQDs; (b) GB-CQDs; (c) PL-CQDs; (d) MF-CQDs. (inset a–d, the photographs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right)).
MF-CQDs: (a) The fluorescence intensity of biomass CQDs in different pH solutions. (b) Photostability test of the CQDs in 360 nm UV lamp under.

MF-CQDs: (a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of aqueous CQDs solution in the presence of various metal ions and anions. (b) The fluorescence spectra of the CQDs solution upon after various concentrations of Fe3+ addition. (c) fluorescence intensity with the addition of Fe3+ aqueous solution in a range of 0.2−100 μM. (d) Fitting curve of the fluorescence intensity versus Fe3+ concentration from 0.2−1 μM.
(a) The TEM image of aggregates of MF-CQDs/Fe3+; (b) PL lifetime decays curves of MF-CQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+.
(a–d) Fluorescence intensity of aqueous CQDs with or without interference metals (1 mM Fe3+, 1 mM of other metal ion).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of biomass CQDs from biomass waste by hydrothermal treatment and the mechanism of Fe3+ detection with as-prepared CQDs.
Comparison of performance of the biomass CQDs sensor with other fluorescent Fe3+ probes.
PL lifetimes of MF-CQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+.
Detection of Fe3+ in pond water samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of novel carbon quantum dots from biomass waste for highly sensitive detection of iron ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ankang University, Ankang, 725000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ankang University, Ankang 725000, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, some common and inexpensive biomass waste were used as carbon sources including orange peel, ginkgo biloba leaves, paulownia leaves and magnolia flower to prepare biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by using a facile hydrothermal process. These biomass CQDs exhibited the advantages of homogeneous particle size, superior water solubility and stability, similar optical properties and surface functional group. The fluorescence quenching effect was observed when Fe3+ ions were added into CQDs solution. The experiment results showed that the as-prepared CQDs have a sensitive response to Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 0.2−100 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) could as low as 0.073 μM. More importantly, practical applications for the detection Fe3+ ions in pond water acquired satisfactory recoveries (94 %–108 %). Therefore, this work not only provided a new way for reusages of biomass waste, but also provided a novel alternative sensor for Fe3+ ions detection in real-world.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most abundant trace element in biological systems, Fe3+ plays essential roles in human bodies [1]. It is closely related to oxygen transport, cellular metabolism and DNA synthesis processes due to its facile redox chemistry and high affinity for oxygen [2,3]. However, abnormal level of Fe3+ ions will damage the normal operation of organism, which could lead to the occurrence of some diseases, such as anemia, hemochromatosis, hepatitis, arthritis, diabetes, heart failure and cancer [4–7]. Therefore, the determination of Fe3+ is important for the early identification and prevention of these diseases. Up to now, there are several methods have been developed for the detection of Fe3+ ions, including atomic absorption spectrometry, Spectrophotometry inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), electrochemical method [8–11]. However, these detection assays require sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures, which greatly limits their practical applications in the determination of Fe3+ ions. Recently, the quantum dots (QDs) fluorescence probe provides a better choice for the determination of Fe3+ ions due to its unique advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, low-cost and easy-to-operate. Nevertheless, most of semiconductor quantum dots fluorescence probe contain heavy metals such as cadmium (CdSe QDs, CdTe QDs) and lead (PbS QDs, CsPbBr3 QDs), which is not conducive to safe detection in biological systems [12–14]. Therefore, a novel metal-free QDs fluorescence probe with excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity is urgent developed.

As a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials family, CQDs have been widely used as fluorescent probes in the detection of metal ions [15–18], biomolecules and small organic molecules [19–21], owing to their excellent material properties such as stable photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and good dispersibility in water [22–25]. For example, In 2018, Liu et al. [26] prepared the S,N-CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, thiourea and acetone for detection of Fe3+ ions in vitro with a linear response ranging of 0.3 mM-0.5 mM, LOD was 0.1 mmol. In the same year, Khalid et al. [27] prepared P, N-CQDs with high selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+ ions by hydrothermal method using citric acid, urea and phosphoric acid as raw materials in dimethylformamide solution. The detection range was 0.1- 0.9 μM with a detection limit of 0.05 μM. In 2019, Tan et al. [28] obtained F-CQDs with uniform size by one-step hydrothermal treatment of perfluorooctanoic sulfonated, which was used for label-free detection of Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. However, these synthetic methods involve toxic or relatively expensive materials, which limit the large-scale development of CQDs. Therefore, it is extremely urgent to find some green, safe and cheap carbon sources. Biomass waste, such as leaves and peels, as renewable and low-cost carbon sources are often discarded in our daily life, which resulted in the massive waste of this resource. If this resource can be reused, it will create more meaningful value. So far, a small amount of work has been done. In 2013, Sun et al. prepared water-soluble photoluminescent CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of low-cost willow bark and used them in photocatalysts [29]. In 2016, the Gupta group prepared blue fluorescent CQDs from lemon peel waste and applied it to sensing and photocatalysis [30]. Therefore, in recent years, biomass waste has gradually become the most attractive carbon source for CQDs synthesis due to the advantages of green and economical.

In this work, four kinds of blue fluorescence biomass CQDs were prepared by facile and eco-friendly one-pot hydrothermal treated with the orange peel, ginkgo biloba leaves, paulownia leaves and magnolia flowers as the raw. The obtained four CQDs were denoted as OP-CQDs, GB-CQDs, PL-CQDs and MF-CQDs, respectively. The as-prepared CQDs showed a sensitive response to Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 0.2−100 μM, and the LOD were as low as 0.073 μM, 0.080 μM, 0.099 μM and 0.088 μM, respectively. This reported CQDs fluorescent sensor was further successfully applied to detect Fe3+ ions in pond water, and the satisfactory recoveries (94 %–108 %) were obtained, which presented potential applications in the actual environment.

Copper chloride and ferric nitrate were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Ltd. Zinc acetate and Cadmium chloride were acquired from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid and anhydrous magnesium sulfate were got from Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was acquired from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China). Potassium chloride were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent III. Silver nitrate was purchased from Guangzhou Jinhuada Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Chromium nitrate and Lead chloride were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory. Sodium chloride, ferrous sulfate, aluminum nitrate and barium chloride were supplied by Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Mercury chloride from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Purchasing Supply Station distributor. Anhydrous calcium chloride was got from Hedong District Hongyan Reagent Factory. Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid was obtained from Wuhan Hongshan Zhongnan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was prepared with a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). Orange peel comes from fresh oranges in the fruit shop. Ginkgo biloba leaves, paulownia leaves and magnolia flowers come from sycamore trees, ginkgo trees and magnolia trees on campus respectively. All chemical reagents were analytical grades and without further purification.

The microstructure information of CQDs was acquired using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, TF20, JoeL 2100 F) at 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra were analyzed by an F-7000 spectrofluorometer with excitation and emission slit widths of 1 nm and 1 nm, respectively. The PL lifetime was measured using an Edinburgh FLS 1000, F-7000. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) information was detected with a Rigaku D/MAX 3C powder diffractometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired on the BRUKER VERTEX70 FT-IR spectrometer in a range of 4000–400 cm−1 using KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by using a Kratos AXIS NOVA multi-function imaging electron spectrometer.

The biomass waste carbon sources are listed in Fig. S1. The preparation process of CQDs takes MF-CQDs as an example, the magnolia flower was washed with distilled water, and placed in an oven at 60 ℃ until it was completely dried, then pulverized into a powder. 0.5 g of powder was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, 50 mL of ultrapure water was added, and the reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 8 h. After, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution was filtered to remove large particle impurities, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The purification of the MF-CQDs was conducted through a dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h. The other three CQDs were prepared in the same manner as above.

Fluorescence QY of the CQDs were measured by using quinine sulfate (QY 54 %) as a reference. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid aqueous solution [31]. By using the formula of
φ x
=
φ s
×
A s
A x
×
I x
I s
×
η x
2
η s
2
, where η , I , A and φ represented the refractive index of the solvent, integral area of fluorescence intensity, absorbance and quantum yield, respectively. The subscript s and x represented the reference and object, respectively.

The detection of Fe3+ ions was carried out in an aqueous solution at room temperature. A series of concentrations Fe3+ ions aqueous solutions (0–100 μM) were prepared in advance and added to an aqueous solution containing the same concentration of CQDs. The PL spectrum was recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer at the optimum excitation wavelength.

The functional groups on the surface of CQDs were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1c, these CQDs exhibited similar FTIR spectra, thus revealed the existence of similar chemical compositions on the surface of CQDs. The stretching vibrations of OH and NH at 3330 cm−1, CH at 2923 cm−1, CO at 1597 cm−1 [36], CN at 1391 cm−1 [37], COC at 1069 cm−1 [38], and CS at 665 cm−1 [39], were observed in all of CQDs. Meanwhile, the XPS spectrum of the MF-CQDs was showed in Fig. 1d, it can be seen that the MF-CQDs consist of carbon (C 1s, 284.6 eV), nitrogen (N 1s, 399.4eV), oxygen (O 1s, 532.4 eV) and sulfur (S 2p, 163.4 eV). The deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2 a) revealed peaks at 284.5, 285.6 and 286.2 and 288.1 eV, respectively corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO bonding on the surface of MF-CQDs [40]. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2b) confirmed the presence of CNC (399 eV), NH (399.7 eV) and CNC (400.7 eV) [37]. The O 1s spectra presented in Fig. 2c could be split into two peaks at 531.5 and 532.6 eV, which indicated the existence of CO and CO [41]. The S 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2d) could be divided into three peaks at 162.2, 163.2 and 168.3 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2 and SO, respectively [42,43]. Based on the FTIR and XPS spectra, it can conclude that the surface of CQDs has hydrophilic groups such as −OH, −COOH and –NH2, which imparted an excellent water solubility to the CQDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of all the CQDs (Fig. 3 ) in aqueous solution showed a broad absorption edge in the UV region, which were attributed to various n–π* (CO) and π–π* (CC) transitions [44]. The maximum excitation wavelength of OP-CQDs (Fig. 3a), GB-CQDs (Fig. 3b), PL-CQDs (Fig. 3c), and MF-CQDs (Fig. 3d) were at 360 nm, 360 nm, 345 nm and 350 nm, respectively. The maximum emission wavelengths were at 445 nm, 435 nm, 435 nm and 435 nm, respectively. The fluorescence emission peak of all CQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior which was consistent with most reports [45]. As shown in Fig. S3, with the increased of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peaks red-shifted and the fluorescence intensity was increased firstly and then decreased gradually. This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior may be ascribed to CQDs with the different emission sites and particle sizes [46]. Besides, the QY of the prepared biomass CQDs were 4.29 % (OP-CQDs), 7.72 % (GB-CQDs), 4.74 % (PL-CQDs) and 8.13 % (MF-CQDs), respectively.

To investigate the luminescence mechanism and the applications of CQDs, the pH is the crucial factor that must be considered. Herein, the fluorescence emission intensities of the CQDs were monitored at maximum excitation wavelength in CQDs aqueous solutions with pH values ranging from 2 to 12 (Fig. 4 a). It was observed that the fluorescence emission of all CQDs were sensitive to pH. As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of all the CQDs were high in acidic and neutral solution, whereas the fluorescence was markedly quenched in alkaline solution. This pH-sensitive behavior may be attributed to the CQDs were agglomerated when pH increased [47]. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence emission peak red-shifted with the increase of pH, which confirmed the conjecture that CQDs had agglomerated. With the increased of pH, non-covalent bond interactions such as hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino were enhanced, which may be the cause of aggregation of CQDs [47–49]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the CQDs were dispersed in ultrapure water solution were irradiated for 8 h under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was not greatly reduced, which proved that the prepared biomass CQDs have excellent optical stability. Furthermore, to further the study the stability of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was collected in various concentrations salt solutions. As Fig. S5 shows, although in high concentration (1 M NaCl) salt solution, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs remained unchanged, indicative of its potential applications as an optical probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CQDs to Fe3+ ions and fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The above characterization proved that the biomass CQDs contain amino groups, hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups on their surface, which can interact with the detection object. And we found biomass CQDs could be selected as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective determination of Fe3+. Firstly, the fluorescent quenching of the CQDs was studied with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5 a, it can be seen that the fluorescent intensity of the biomass CQDs was gradually decayed with the concentrations of Fe3+ ions increasing, which can be further verified by the fluorescence photographs of CQDs was gradually fading under the illumination of 365 nm UV lamb as shown in Fig. S6. The relationship of the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe3+ ions were well confirmed to the exponential function (y = 0.9637-0.9976/(1+(x/5.8340)0.66) with good correlation (Fig. 5b, 0.2−100 μM R2 = 0.99401). Here, the y and x referred to (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+ ions (μM), respectively. Besides, a better linearity can be achieved at a low Fe3+ ions concentration range. As shown in Fig. 5c, when the Fe3+ ions concentration range was reduced to 0.2−1 μM, the fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ ions concentration had a good linear relationship: y = 0.01248 + 0.22016x, R2 = 0.98568.

The limit of detection of MF-CQDs system for Fe3+ ions was measured to be 0.088 μM. Similarly, OP-CDs (Fig. S7, LOD = 0.073 μM), GB-CQDs (Fig. S8, LOD = 0.080 μM) and PL-CQDs (Fig. S9, LOD = 0.10 μM) had similar characteristics of Fe3+ ions sensitivity. Further, in order to investigate the selectivity of biomass CQDs for Fe3+ ions, we performed fluorescence measurements in the presence of various metal ions (1 mM) such as Ag+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ba2+ and Hg2+. All experiments were carried out in an environment of pH = 6 (the original pH of the CQDs solution). As shown in Fig. 5d, it was noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of MF-CQDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions, while the influence of other metal ions was almost negligible. Compared with some other fluorescent Fe3+ ions probes as shown in Table1 . It can be seen that the biomass CQDs Fe3+ ions sensor showed better or comparable performances, including the detection limit, detection range and quantum yield.

To understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the TEM (Fig. 6 a) of MF-CQDs in the present of Fe3+ were investigated and the PL lifetime decays (Fig. 6b) of MF-CQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 6a, the MF-CQDs were significantly agglomerated in the present of Fe3+, due to the fact that the strong affinity interaction between Fe3+ ions and −COOH, -NH2 and −OH of CQDs leads to the aggregation of CQDs [50–52]. The agglomerated CQDs facilitated the recombination of non-radiative electrons and holes, and then caused the fluorescence quenching due to the fact that the outer elec-tronic structure of Fe3+ is 4s23d5 and the five d orbits are half-filled, the electrons in the excited state of CQDs will transfer to the 3d orbits of Fe3+ [53,54]. It was reported that the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of CQDs belongs to static quenching [55]. Therefore, here, the fluorescence lifetime decays curves were used to study the fluorescence quenching process of MF-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6b, the measures results were shown in Table 2 , the PL lifetimes of the MF-CQDs was displayed no obviously change in the presence and absence of Fe3+, implying that the quenching process involves static quenching.

In order to explore interface effect of other metal ions in Fe3+ ions detection, the following control experiment were designed: 1 mM of individual Fe3+ ions (red bars, Fig. 7 ) and the mixtures of 1 mM of Fe3+ and 1 mM of the above mentioned metal ions (blue bars, Fig. 7) were respectively added into the CQDs aqueous solution, then the fluorescence of the CQDs were detected. The above results proved that the coexistence of other metal ions only has a little or negligible effect on the fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate the potential practical applications of the CQDs in the detection of Fe3+ ion. Before test, the pond water was collected from Northwest University and then was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then the Fe3+ in pond water was tested by the CQDs fluorescence used as sensors. As shown in Table 3 , the Fe3+ concentration of pond water is 0.39 μM and the obtained recoveries range from 94.5%–108%, which indicated the usefulness of the CQDs for the detection of Fe3+ in water samples. Therefore, the biomass CQDs fluorescence sensors shown a promising prospective application for Fe3+ ions detection in actual samples and accompanied with the versatile advantages such as convenient operation and reliable measurement results.

In summary, the four kinds of biomass wastes were used for the preparation of CQDs. The prepared biomass CQDs with monodisperse particles and uniform size (average size is 2.60 nm). And these CQDs exhibited bright blue-luminescence and excellent stability. Furthermore, the biomass CQDs were used as fluorescence sensors for the detection of Fe3+ ions with high sensitivity and selectivity. The detection limit was as low as 0.073 μM. Finally, this sensor was successfully applied for the detection of Fe3+ in pond water and obtained satisfactory recoveries (94 %–108 %). This work not only provided a promising CQDs fluorescence sensor for detection of Fe3+, but also paved a new avenue for exploring the biomass waste for practical application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that no conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476183 and 21676213), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China ((2014JM2-2012), the Special Funds for Scientific Research of High-level Talents of Ankang University (2015AYQDZR02), and the Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Education Department (Nos. 18JK0014 and 19JS003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2019.110730.
